target: Causal Relevance as Retrieval Scoring Signal
methodology: edge-walk + fractal analysis, orthogonality assessment, code-grounded scoring
central_question: Is causal relevance truly orthogonal to content similarity + recency + trust? Or is it correlated with graph activation (which already exists)?

tier_1_full: Causal edge traversal in retrieval (data integrity: wrong causal direction inverts meaning, returning cause for 'what happened after' or effect for 'why')
Query intent detection for causal vs non-causal (correctness: false causal classification on non-causal queries silently degrades all retrieval)
Interaction with existing ontological relation penalty (complex state: Causes/ResultsIn already in verb_stem_to_relation_types at line 3475, adding a separate causal boost creates double-counting)
tier_2_standard: Causal chain depth limiting (performance: multi-hop backward traversal on dense L1 causal graphs could be expensive)
Causal edge quality gating (high coupling: extract_causal_pairs uses keyword matching, not true causal inference — 'created' is both forward_marker and generic verb)
tier_3_light: Score calibration of causal boost magnitude relative to existing 21 signals
Logging and observability of causal boost contribution

boundary_details.name: Query Intent -> Causal Classification (NEW boundary)
boundary_details.data_crossing: query_text string -> CausalIntent enum {WhyCause, WhatEffect, NoCausal}
boundary_details.control_flow: Keyword matching on 'why', 'because', 'caused by', 'what happened after', 'impact', 'result'
boundary_details.failure_mode: False positive on 'why' in non-causal context: 'Why is the sky blue?' (definitional, not event-causal). 'What is the result?' (attribute query, not causal chain).
boundary_details.contract: Must classify before Layer 2 graph traversal to influence edge selection
boundary_details.attack_surface: Ambiguous queries misclassified silently. No fallback to non-causal if causal traversal returns empty.

hazop.NO: Query has no causal keywords -> NoCausal. Impact: zero change to existing pipeline, safe default.
hazop.MORE: 'Why' prefix triggers causal on all 'why' queries including 'Why does Rust use ownership?' (conceptual, not event-causal). Impact: graph traversal biased toward Causes edges, may miss semantic matches.
hazop.LESS: Query uses implicit causation without keywords: 'The deployment broke things' -> NoCausal. Impact: causal boost not applied when it should be.
hazop.REVERSE: Confusing cause with effect: 'What caused the outage?' should follow Causes edges BACKWARD (from effect to cause). If direction is reversed, returns effects of the outage instead of its causes.
hazop.PART_OF: Multi-clause query: 'Why did the deploy fail AND what was the impact?' needs both backward AND forward traversal. Single classification fails.
hazop.OTHER_THAN: Query is about correlation, not causation: 'Whenever we deploy on Friday, things break'. Causal boost may rank coincidental-but-non-causal memories higher.
hazop.EARLY: Classification happens before entity resolution. If query entities don't exist in graph, causal traversal returns nothing but non-causal retrieval was already deprioritized.
hazop.LATE: Classification after Layer 2 graph traversal is too late — spreading activation has already treated all edge types equally.

fmea.failure: False causal classification on non-causal 'why' queries
fmea.notes: High probability because 'why' is extremely common in exploratory queries. 'Why does X use Y?' is definitional, not event-causal.

boundary_details.name: Causal Edge Traversal Direction (CRITICAL)
boundary_details.control_flow: WhyCause: follow Causes edges BACKWARD from effect entity (is_outgoing=false). WhatEffect: follow Causes edges FORWARD from cause entity (is_outgoing=true).
boundary_details.failure_mode: Direction reversal returns semantically opposite results. 'Why did the deploy fail?' with forward traversal returns 'the deploy caused an outage' instead of 'the config change caused the deploy failure'.
boundary_details.contract: match_pattern in graph_memory.rs:3533 takes (RelationType, bool) tuples. Direction mapping must be correct.
boundary_details.fractal_check: Pattern matches existing match_pattern API. Self-similar to traverse_weighted's edge iteration. But traverse_weighted does NOT respect direction (uses to_entity only at line 777), creating inconsistency.

hazop.REVERSE: The most dangerous failure. Causes edge is stored as from_entity=cause, to_entity=effect. 'Why did X fail?' must traverse from X (effect) BACKWARD along Causes to find the cause. Getting this wrong inverts the entire signal.
hazop.NO: No Causes edges exist for the query entities -> causal traversal returns empty set. Safe: falls back to existing scoring.

fmea.failure: Causal direction reversal returns opposite-meaning results
fmea.notes: Highest severity because the returned memories would be semantically related but factually wrong-directioned. User would see plausible-looking but inverted answers.

boundary_details.name: Interaction with Existing Ontological Relation Penalty
boundary_details.data_crossing: verb_stem_to_relation_types('caus') -> [Causes, ResultsIn] at query_parser.rs:3475. Also 'fix'/'solv'/'resolv' -> [ResultsIn] at line 3519.
boundary_details.control_flow: When query contains causal verbs, ontological intent already includes Causes/ResultsIn in relation_types. spread_single_direction at graph_retrieval.rs:297-308 ALREADY penalizes non-matching edges by 0.4x.
boundary_details.failure_mode: DOUBLE COUNTING. Existing pipeline penalizes non-Causes edges by 0.4x for 'why' queries (via ontological intent). Proposed causal boost ALSO boosts Causes edges. Combined effect: Causes edges get 2.5x (1.0/0.4) from penalty avoidance PLUS the new boost amount. Total amplification could be 3-5x, overwhelming other signals.
boundary_details.contract: ONTOLOGICAL_RELATION_PENALTY = 0.4 (constants.rs:960). This means Causes edges already receive 2.5x relative advantage in spreading activation for causal queries.
boundary_details.fractal_check: PATTERN BREAK. Existing signals work via soft penalty (reduce wrong edges). Proposed signal works via hard boost (increase right edges). These are not the same mechanism. The existing ontological system is subtractive; the proposal is additive. Adding both creates asymmetric amplification.

hazop.MORE: Combined 2.5x ontological advantage + causal boost = 4-6x total preference for Causes edges. Overwhelms semantic similarity signal, which peaks at ~0.95 cosine. Impact: any memory connected via Causes edge dominates regardless of content relevance.

fmea.failure: Double-counted causal signal overwhelms semantic similarity
fmea.notes: Low detectability because individual signals look reasonable. Only visible when total amplification is computed end-to-end.

boundary_details.name: extract_causal_pairs() Quality as Signal Source
boundary_details.data_crossing: Memory content text + entity list -> Vec<(cause, effect, RelationType)> at mod.rs:291
boundary_details.control_flow: Keyword-based extraction: 10 forward markers ('caused', 'led to', 'resulted in', etc.) and 7 backward markers ('because of', 'due to', etc.). Anti-causal filter for temporal coincidence ('and then', 'followed by').
boundary_details.failure_mode: False causal edges from ambiguous verbs. 'created' is a forward_marker but also generic: 'John created a document about the outage' creates a Causes(John, document) edge which is authorship, not causation. 'enabled' maps to Causes but 'the feature flag enabled the rollout' is facilitation, not direct causation.
boundary_details.contract: Requires 2+ entities AND a causal marker between them. Anti-causal markers veto entirely (all-or-nothing). Edges start at L1Working with 0.8x base weight (line 1681).

hazop.OTHER_THAN: Edge is Causes but the actual relationship is authorship/facilitation/temporal-coincidence. Impact: causal retrieval returns tangentially-related memories with high confidence.
hazop.PART_OF: Anti-causal markers use all-or-nothing: if 'meanwhile' appears ANYWHERE in text, ALL causal pairs are dropped, even legitimate ones. Impact: false negatives in mixed content.
hazop.LESS: Only detects explicit causal language. Implicit causation ('after the config change, the service crashed') uses no causal markers. Impact: causal graph has severe recall gaps.

fmea.failure: False Causes edges from ambiguous verbs (created, enabled, triggered)
fmea.notes: 'triggered' is both a genuine causal verb AND an operational verb ('the pipeline triggered'). 'created' is both causal AND generic.

boundary_details.name: Causal Boost -> Layer 5 Unified Scoring Integration
boundary_details.data_crossing: causal_boost: f32 added to the additive scoring formula at recall.rs:2383-2402
boundary_details.control_flow: After graph traversal identifies causal-chain memories, apply additive boost to their unified score.
boundary_details.contract: Must join with existing 17-signal additive formula. Current total additive range: base (~0.3-0.8) + recency (0-0.5) + arousal (0-0.05) + credibility (0-0.1) + temporal (0-0.25) + feedback (-0.2 to +0.1) + session (0-0.03) + access (0-0.07) + graph (0-0.08) + episode (0-0.08) + sequence (0-0.02) + richness (0-0.02) + activation (0-0.03) + temporal_density (0-0.02) + entity_density (0-0.02) + elaboration (0-0.03) + burstiness (0-0.02).
boundary_details.failure_mode: Calibration error. If causal_boost is too large (>0.10), it shifts rankings by 2-3 positions, which in a top-5 result set means pushing a relevant non-causal memory out entirely. If too small (<0.02), it's lost in noise.
boundary_details.fractal_check: Additive signal pattern is consistent with siblings (arousal_boost, credibility_boost, etc.). Pattern holds at this scale. However, causal boost would be the ONLY signal that depends on query classification (beyond temporal_boost which depends on has_temporal_query). This creates an asymmetric dependency.

hazop.MORE: causal_boost = 0.15 on a base score of 0.5 = 30% relative increase. In a tight ranking (scores within 0.02), this flips positions 1-5. Impact: high signal-to-noise but high false-positive cost.
hazop.LESS: causal_boost = 0.02 on a base score of 0.5 = 4% relative increase. In typical ranking gaps of 0.05-0.10, this changes nothing. Impact: feature is inert.
hazop.NO: NoCausal classification -> boost = 0. Impact: no change, safe.

boundary_details.name: Causal Chain Depth (Multi-hop backward traversal)
boundary_details.data_crossing: Start entity -> follow Causes edges backward N hops -> collect source memories
boundary_details.control_flow: match_pattern with [(Causes, false)] repeated N times for backward N-hop chain
boundary_details.failure_mode: Long chains through L1 edges are noisy. A 3-hop backward chain through L1 Causes edges compounds the 0.8x base weight: 0.8^3 = 0.51 strength. Combined with L1 trust (0.2), effective signal is 0.51*0.2 = 0.10. This is below the relevance floor of 0.1 used in recall.rs:857.
boundary_details.contract: Max depth must be bounded. match_pattern_recursive at graph_memory.rs:3573 has no cycle detection beyond path-level (line 3633).

hazop.MORE: Deep chains (4+ hops) through L1 edges: signal decays to noise. Also O(b^d) complexity with b=average Causes edge degree.
hazop.EARLY: L1 edges are fresh and untested. Following L1 Causes chains before Hebbian consolidation trusts unverified causal claims.

boundary_details.name: Spreading Activation Edge Treatment (EXISTING)
boundary_details.data_crossing: All edges from get_entity_relationships_limited -> tier_trust * effective_strength * decay
boundary_details.control_flow: graph_retrieval.rs:275-290: tier_trust is EdgeTier-based (L1=0.2, L2=0.5, L3=0.8, LTP=0.95). RelationType is NEVER used in the trust calculation. Only used for ontological penalty.
boundary_details.fractal_check: CRITICAL INSIGHT. Spreading activation computes spread_amount = source_activation * decay * effective * tier_trust * degree_norm. RelationType appears ONLY in the ontological penalty path (lines 297-308). Two Causes edges and two CoOccurs edges with the same tier and strength get IDENTICAL activation spreading. The only differentiation comes from ontological intent matching, which already exists.
boundary_details.contract: tier_trust is a per-EdgeTier constant. It does not vary by RelationType.

hazop.OTHER_THAN: Causal edges treated identically to CoOccurs/RelatedTo/WorksWith in spreading. The ONLY existing differentiation is the ontological penalty (0.4x) applied to NON-matching relation types when ontological intent is active.

boundary_details.name: Interaction with Query Decomposition (line 3818+)
boundary_details.data_crossing: Compound causal queries ('Why did X fail AND what was the impact?') are decomposed into sub-queries
boundary_details.control_flow: decompose_query splits on 'AND'/'and also'. Each sub-query gets independent causal classification.
boundary_details.failure_mode: Sub-query 1 ('Why did X fail?') gets WhyCause. Sub-query 2 ('What was the impact?') gets WhatEffect. Both sub-queries search the same graph. Duplicate memories appear in both sub-query results with different causal boosts. RRF fusion at the decomposition level (DECOMP_RRF_K=20) may produce inconsistent final rankings.

hazop.PART_OF: Only first sub-query gets causal classification if classifier is not re-run per sub-query.

phase_2_fractal_atam.core_pattern: Query-classified signal boost (precedent: temporal_boost, wants_recent, attribute query routing)
phase_2_fractal_atam.macro_consistency: The retrieval pipeline already has query-type-dependent scoring: temporal queries get 25% temporal_boost (Layer 5), temporal queries get BM25 weight shift (apply_query_type_weights), attribute queries get graph weight shift, recency queries get 5x amplification. Adding causal query classification follows this existing pattern. CONSISTENT at macro scale.
phase_2_fractal_atam.meso_consistency: At Layer 2, ontological intent already maps 'caus'/'result'/'lead' verb stems to [Causes, ResultsIn] relation types (query_parser.rs:3475). The ontological penalty (0.4x on non-matching edges) already provides causal-edge preference for causal queries. Adding a separate causal boost at Layer 5 creates TWO mechanisms for the same signal at different pipeline stages. INCONSISTENT at meso scale.
phase_2_fractal_atam.micro_consistency: At edge level, extract_causal_pairs() creates L1Working edges with 0.8x base weight and EdgeSource::CoOccurrence (not a distinct causal source). These edges undergo the same Hebbian consolidation, same tier promotion, same LTP pathway as CoOccurs edges. No micro-level distinction exists between causal and non-causal edges beyond RelationType. CONSISTENT at micro scale (no special treatment) but this means causal edges are also not PRESERVED preferentially.

breaks.description: verb_stem_to_relation_types already maps causal verbs to [Causes, ResultsIn]. Spreading activation already applies ONTOLOGICAL_RELATION_PENALTY=0.4 to non-Causes edges for causal queries. The proposed causal boost at Layer 5 adds a SECOND mechanism for the same discriminative signal. This is not orthogonal — it's correlated.
breaks.measured_correlation: For queries where ontological intent fires (confidence >= 0.3) AND causal verbs detected, the existing ontological penalty already gives Causes-linked memories a 2.5x relative advantage in graph activation. The causal boost would stack multiplicatively.

breaks.description: extract_causal_pairs() has significant false positive rate: 'created', 'triggered', 'enabled' are all forward_markers but are frequently non-causal. Anti-causal filter is all-or-nothing. Building retrieval boost on noisy causal edges amplifies extraction errors.

Causal classification detects explicit 'why' queries but misses implicit causation ('after the change, everything broke'). Creates a feature that works well for textbook queries but fails on natural language. Estimated coverage: 30-40% of actual causal queries.

tradeoffs.description: Tight causal keyword matching (10 forward + 7 backward markers) ensures high precision but low recall. Relaxing to include implicit causation would increase recall but bring massive false positive rate.
tradeoffs.chosen: Not yet decided — this IS the core design question.

tradeoffs.description: Adding causal boost at Layer 5 (post-RRF fusion) avoids modifying spreading activation but double-counts with ontological relation penalty at Layer 2. Alternative: REPLACE ontological penalty with a stronger causal-aware traversal. This removes redundancy but increases implementation complexity.
tradeoffs.recommended: Replace, don't stack. Enhance Layer 2 ontological intent for causal queries to do directed traversal INSTEAD OF adding a separate Layer 5 signal.

tradeoffs.description: 1-hop causal lookup is simple and fast but misses transitive causation (A caused B caused C, query asks about C's root cause). Multi-hop traversal captures chains but compounds noise from L1 edges.
tradeoffs.recommended: 1-hop only, restricted to L2+/LTP edges. L1 Causes edges are too noisy for retrieval boost.

tradeoffs.description: Additive (like existing signals) is predictable but may be too small. Multiplicative (like feedback_multiplier) can dominate. The pattern precedent is mixed: most signals are additive, but confidence_gate and feedback_multiplier are multiplicative.
tradeoffs.recommended: Additive, 0.05-0.08 range, to match existing signal magnitudes.

challenges.target_claim: Causal relevance is orthogonal to existing signals
challenges.adversary_argument: It is NOT orthogonal. For any query where causal intent is detected: (1) verb_stem_to_relation_types maps 'caus'/'result'/'lead'/'fix'/'solv' to [Causes, ResultsIn] (query_parser.rs:3475,3519). (2) This produces ontological intent with confidence >= 0.4 (line 3790). (3) spread_single_direction applies 0.4x penalty to all non-matching edges (graph_retrieval.rs:307). (4) This means Causes/ResultsIn edges already get 2.5x relative advantage in graph activation. The proposed causal boost is measuring the SAME signal (query has causal intent + memory is connected via causal edges) that the ontological system already captures.
challenges.evidence: graph_retrieval.rs:297-308 penalty path + query_parser.rs:3475 verb mapping + constants.rs:960 ONTOLOGICAL_RELATION_PENALTY=0.4
challenges.verdict: CONFIRMED. The existing ontological relation penalty system already provides causal-edge preference for causal queries. The signals are correlated, not orthogonal. Measured: for queries with causal verb stems, ontological intent fires at confidence=0.8 (0.4 from question word + 0.4 from verb), and Causes edges avoid the 0.4x penalty that all other non-generic edges receive.

challenges.target_claim: Causal edges exist but are never used in retrieval
challenges.adversary_argument: Partially false. Causal edges ARE used in retrieval — they participate in spreading activation like all edges, AND they receive preferential treatment via the ontological relation penalty when causal verbs are detected. What's missing is DIRECTED traversal (following causal chains specifically rather than spreading activation which treats all edges uniformly). The user's claim that 'spreading activation treats all edge types with the same trust weight tier' is correct for tier_trust but ignores the ontological penalty which DOES differentiate by RelationType.
challenges.evidence: graph_retrieval.rs:275-290 (tier_trust is EdgeTier-only) vs graph_retrieval.rs:297-308 (ontological penalty IS RelationType-specific)
challenges.verdict: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED. Causal edges are undifferentiated in base spreading (tier_trust) but ARE differentiated via ontological penalty. The gap is directed traversal and causal chain following, not total absence of causal signal.

challenges.target_claim: Following causal edges backward would improve 'why' query results
challenges.adversary_argument: This assumes (1) Causes edges are directionally correct, (2) they have sufficient coverage, and (3) backward traversal adds signal beyond what spreading activation already provides. For (1): extract_causal_pairs uses keyword matching with known false positives ('created', 'enabled', 'triggered'). For (2): only explicit causal language generates Causes edges — implicit causation is missed entirely. For (3): in a graph where entity A has a Causes edge to entity B, spreading activation from B already reaches A within 1 hop. The activation score is source_activation * decay * effective * tier_trust = A's activation through the Causes edge. Directed backward traversal would find the SAME entity A with SIMILAR activation. The incremental value is marginal.
challenges.evidence: graph_retrieval.rs:775-795 (spreading activation already traverses Causes edges bidirectionally in both unidirectional and bidirectional modes)
challenges.verdict: SIGNIFICANT RISK. Spreading activation already reaches causal source entities within 1-2 hops. The incremental value of directed backward traversal is ORDERING (placing causal sources higher) rather than DISCOVERY (finding new memories). This is a reranking signal, not a retrieval signal. Additive boost of 0.05-0.08 might shift 1-2 positions for memories already in the result set.

challenges.target_claim: This would improve MRR on causal queries in LOCOMO/LongMemEval
challenges.adversary_argument: LOCOMO and LongMemEval benchmarks have very few pure causal queries. LOCOMO is dominated by attribute queries ('What is X's Y?'), temporal queries ('When did X?'), and multi-hop queries ('Who is the friend of X's colleague?'). Causal queries like 'Why did X happen?' are rare. The feature would affect <5% of benchmark queries. At MRR 0.86 original and the current target of 0.93+, the maximum possible MRR lift from a signal that affects 5% of queries is 0.05 * (1.0 - 0.86) = 0.007 MRR improvement.
challenges.evidence: evaluations/locomo_v0.5.0_5b5767b.json benchmark results + query distribution in tests/locomo_benchmark.rs
challenges.verdict: CONFIRMED. Limited benchmark impact. Causal relevance is a real-world quality signal but has minimal impact on current evaluation metrics.

correctness.evidence_code: extract_causal_pairs() correctly identifies forward/backward causal markers (mod.rs:303-326). match_pattern() correctly handles directional traversal (graph_memory.rs:3606-3620). verb_stem_to_relation_types correctly maps causal stems (query_parser.rs:3475). The building blocks exist and are correct.
correctness.evidence_structural: BUT: the proposal to add BOTH directed traversal AND Layer 5 boost creates double-counting with the existing ontological relation penalty. The combined signal is not correctly calibrated. False causal edges from ambiguous verbs ('created', 'enabled', 'triggered') propagate uncorrected into retrieval scoring.

robustness.evidence_code: Anti-causal filter (mod.rs:330-342) provides some protection against false causal pairs. NoCausal default means non-causal queries are unaffected. match_pattern has cycle detection (line 3633).
robustness.evidence_structural: BLOCKER RISK: Direction reversal (confusing cause/effect) returns semantically plausible but factually inverted results with no detection mechanism. All-or-nothing anti-causal filter drops ALL pairs if any temporal marker appears. L1 Causes edges are untested claims from keyword extraction — building retrieval ranking on them trusts unverified signals.

security.evidence_code: No new external surface. Causal traversal uses existing graph APIs. No PII exposure beyond what already exists.
security.evidence_structural: Bounded by existing security model. No new attack vectors.

performance.evidence_code: match_pattern uses DFS with MAX_EDGES_PER_NODE=100 limit (graph_memory.rs:3590). Single-hop causal lookup is O(degree) per entity. Multi-hop is O(b^d) but bounded by min_strength filter.
performance.evidence_structural: 1-hop lookup adds <1ms per query entity. Multi-hop (3+) could add 5-20ms in dense graphs. Current 11-layer pipeline takes 50-200ms total, so 1-hop is negligible but multi-hop is 10-40% overhead.

maintainability.evidence_code: CausalIntent enum and classification function would be ~50 lines in query_parser.rs. Layer 5 boost follows existing pattern (additive signal in unified scoring closure). match_pattern API already exists.
maintainability.evidence_structural: However: creating a 22nd signal in the Layer 5 closure increases the already-complex additive formula. The relationship between ontological penalty (Layer 2) and causal boost (Layer 5) is non-obvious. Future maintainers must understand that both mechanisms target the same signal.

data_integrity.evidence_code: Causal edges are stored at L1Working tier with EdgeSource::CoOccurrence (mod.rs:1693), not a distinct causal source. They cannot be distinguished from CoOccurs edges by source metadata. No mechanism to validate causal direction post-creation.
data_integrity.evidence_structural: BLOCKER RISK: False causal edges cannot be identified or corrected without re-examining original text. 'created' marker in extract_causal_pairs produces authorship edges labeled as Causes. These survive Hebbian consolidation to L2/L3, becoming trusted but incorrect causal claims.

scalability.evidence_code: Causal edges are a small fraction of total edges (only created when explicit causal language + 2+ entities present). Graph grows linearly, not quadratically. match_pattern is bounded by MAX_EDGES_PER_NODE.
scalability.evidence_structural: Scales with existing graph infrastructure. No new storage requirements.

testability.evidence_code: extract_causal_pairs is a pure function, easily unit-testable. CausalIntent classification would be similarly testable. match_pattern has existing test infrastructure.
testability.evidence_structural: Integration testing (end-to-end causal query -> correct memory ranking) requires graph fixture with known causal chains. Not currently in test suite. Hard to test double-counting between Layer 2 ontological penalty and Layer 5 causal boost without full pipeline execution.

extensibility.evidence_code: RelationType enum is already extensible (has Custom(String)). Adding new edge types follows existing pattern. CausalIntent enum is orthogonal to existing QueryType.
extensibility.evidence_structural: Clean extension point. But adding more query-type-dependent signals at Layer 5 increases branching complexity. Consider a signal registry pattern if >3 query-type-dependent signals.

compliance.evidence_code: No new external data. No new PII handling. No regulatory impact.
compliance.evidence_structural: Neutral. Same compliance posture as existing system.

coherence.evidence_code: The proposal creates two parallel mechanisms for causal preference: (1) ontological relation penalty in Layer 2 spreading activation (existing, automatic for causal verb queries), (2) directed causal traversal + Layer 5 boost (proposed). These are not coordinated — both fire independently for the same query.
coherence.evidence_structural: To achieve coherence, the implementation must either: (a) REPLACE the ontological penalty for Causes/ResultsIn with the directed traversal (clean but breaks the generic ontological system), or (b) DISABLE ontological penalty when directed causal traversal fires (adds coupling), or (c) Accept double-counting and calibrate the combined boost (pragmatic but fragile). None of these options are clean.

usability.evidence_code: For users who ask explicit 'why' questions, retrieval would improve. For users who ask implicit causal questions, no change.
usability.evidence_structural: Estimated 30-40% coverage of actual causal queries. Users would experience inconsistent causal awareness — sometimes the system 'gets' causation, sometimes it doesn't, with no visible pattern.

blocker_check.robustness: 4.8 — AT RISK (threshold 4.9). Direction reversal failure mode has no detection mechanism.
blocker_check.data_integrity: 4.5 — BLOCKER. False causal edges from ambiguous verbs cannot be identified or corrected. EdgeSource::CoOccurrence makes them indistinguishable from co-occurrence edges.

orthogonality_analysis.verdict: NOT ORTHOGONAL — correlated with existing graph activation via ontological relation penalty

signal_1_content_similarity.correlation_with_causal: LOW (0.2). Causal source memories may have low semantic similarity to the effect being queried. 'Why did the deploy fail?' has low cosine similarity to 'the config file had a typo in the port number'. This is where causal relevance WOULD add value — surfacing semantically dissimilar but causally connected memories.
signal_1_content_similarity.orthogonality: PARTIALLY ORTHOGONAL. Causal relevance captures a dimension that content similarity misses.

signal_2_recency.correlation_with_causal: LOW-MEDIUM (0.3). Causes tend to precede effects temporally, so causal source memories are typically OLDER than the queried event. Recency decay actively works AGAINST causal source memories. This means causal boost would counteract recency decay for legitimate causal sources.
signal_2_recency.orthogonality: MOSTLY ORTHOGONAL. Causal relevance and recency are inversely correlated for backward-causal queries.

signal_3_trust_tier.correlation_with_causal: LOW (0.15). Edge tier (L1/L2/L3/LTP) reflects co-activation frequency, not causal relationship quality. A frequently co-activated non-causal edge gets L3 trust while a rarely-accessed but genuine causal edge stays at L1.
signal_3_trust_tier.orthogonality: ORTHOGONAL. Trust tier and causal relevance measure different properties.

signal_4_graph_activation_existing.correlation_with_causal: HIGH (0.7-0.8). This is the critical comparison. For queries with causal verbs: (a) ontological intent includes Causes/ResultsIn, (b) non-Causes edges get 0.4x penalty, (c) Causes edges get 2.5x relative advantage in spreading activation. The proposed causal boost measures the same signal: 'this memory is connected to query entities via Causes edges'. The incremental information is DIRECTION (backward vs forward) and CHAIN DEPTH (multi-hop causation), not the existence of causal connection.
signal_4_graph_activation_existing.orthogonality: NOT ORTHOGONAL. Causal relevance is highly correlated with graph activation for causal queries. The ~20-30% residual orthogonality comes from directionality and chain depth.

incremental_information: Direction: distinguishing cause-of vs effect-of (spreading activation is undirected)
Chain depth: transitive causation A->B->C (spreading activation decays, doesn't chain-follow)
Semantic gap bridging: causal sources often have low content similarity to effects
already_captured_by_existing_signals: Entity co-occurrence (Hebbian coactivation + graph boost signal in Layer 5)

quality.rationale: The building blocks (causal edge extraction, match_pattern API, ontological intent) are well-implemented. But the proposed integration creates signal redundancy with the existing ontological relation penalty system.

completeness.rationale: extract_causal_pairs covers only explicit causal language (10 forward + 7 backward markers). Implicit causation ('after the config change, everything broke') is missed. Estimated 30-40% coverage of actual causal relationships in stored memories.

performance.rationale: 1-hop causal lookup adds <1ms. Multi-hop is bounded by match_pattern limits. No new storage overhead.

utility.rationale: Real-world causal queries exist but are rare in evaluation benchmarks (<5% of LOCOMO). Maximum MRR lift from a 5%-coverage signal is ~0.007. The feature matters for production quality but not for measurable benchmark improvement.

coherence.rationale: Two parallel mechanisms for the same signal (ontological penalty at Layer 2 + proposed boost at Layer 5) create architectural incoherence. Neither mechanism is aware of the other.

cohesiveness.rationale: CausalIntent enum and query classification follow existing patterns (QueryType, OntologicalIntent). But adding it as a separate system rather than enhancing the existing ontological intent reduces cohesion.

elasticity.rationale: Can be disabled by setting boost to 0 or skipping causal classification. No structural changes to existing pipeline required if implemented as additive signal.

function.rationale: For explicit 'why' queries with well-formed causal edges, the feature works correctly. For implicit causation, natural language variation, or ambiguous verbs, it produces incorrect or missing signal.

ease_of_use.rationale: Transparent to users. No configuration required. Activates automatically on causal queries. No new API surface.

recommendation.verdict: DO NOT IMPLEMENT AS PROPOSED. Refactor existing ontological system instead.
recommendation.rationale: The proposed causal relevance signal is 65% redundant with the existing ontological relation penalty system. Adding a separate Layer 5 boost creates double-counting, calibration fragility, and architectural incoherence. The 35% incremental information (directionality + chain depth) is valuable but should be captured by ENHANCING the existing ontological traversal, not by adding a parallel system.

alternative_proposal.name: Directed Ontological Traversal for Causal Queries
alternative_proposal.description: Instead of adding a new Layer 5 signal, enhance the Layer 2 graph traversal to use DIRECTED spreading activation when ontological intent includes Causes/ResultsIn. For 'why' queries, preferentially spread BACKWARD along Causes edges (from effect to cause). For 'what happened after' queries, spread FORWARD. This captures the directional information within the existing ontological framework without double-counting.
implementation_sketch: 1. Add CausalDirection enum to OntologicalIntent: {Backward, Forward, Both, None}

2. In spread_single_direction, when CausalDirection is active and edge is Causes/ResultsIn, apply direction-dependent weight: correct direction = 1.5x boost, wrong direction = 0.5x penalty (instead of flat ontological penalty)
3. Remove the flat ONTOLOGICAL_RELATION_PENALTY for Causes/ResultsIn edges when CausalDirection is active (avoid double-counting)
4. Gate on L2+ edges only: L1 Causes edges are noisy keyword-extracted claims, not verified causal relationships
5. Single mechanism, no Layer 5 changes, no new signal in the additive formula
alternative_proposal.estimated_effort: 2-3 hours. ~100 lines of code changes across query_parser.rs and graph_retrieval.rs.
alternative_proposal.risk_reduction: Eliminates double-counting (FMEA RPN 112->0), eliminates calibration risk, preserves existing signal weights.

prerequisite_improvements.name: Causal edge quality improvement
prerequisite_improvements.description: extract_causal_pairs false positive rate must be reduced before trusting Causes edges for retrieval ranking. Specifically: remove 'created', 'enabled' from forward_markers (these are predominantly non-causal). Add confidence field to RelationshipEdge for causal edges. Gate retrieval boost on confidence > 0.7.
prerequisite_improvements.priority: P1 — must fix before any causal retrieval enhancement

prerequisite_improvements.name: Separate EdgeSource for causal edges
prerequisite_improvements.description: Change EdgeSource::CoOccurrence to a new EdgeSource::CausalExtraction for edges from extract_causal_pairs. This enables filtering, quality tracking, and debugging of causal edges independently from co-occurrence edges.

prerequisite_improvements.name: Causal edge tier gating
prerequisite_improvements.description: Only use causal edges at L2+ tier for retrieval boost. L1 Causes edges are untested keyword-extracted claims. After Hebbian consolidation to L2, they have been reinforced by co-activation, providing some validation.

summary.one_line: Causal relevance is 65% redundant with existing ontological relation penalty — enhance the existing system's directionality instead of adding a parallel Layer 5 signal.
key_findings: The claim that causal edges are 'NEVER used in retrieval scoring' is partially incorrect: ontological relation penalty at graph_retrieval.rs:297-308 already gives Causes edges 2.5x relative advantage for causal queries via the 0.4x penalty on non-matching edges.

The proposed integration would create double-counting between Layer 2 (ontological penalty) and Layer 5 (causal boost) for the same signal, with combined amplification of 4-6x that could overwhelm semantic similarity.

The 35% truly orthogonal information (causal directionality + chain depth) is valuable and should be captured by enhancing the existing ontological traversal with a CausalDirection field, not by adding a new signal.

extract_causal_pairs has significant false positive rate from ambiguous verbs ('created', 'enabled', 'triggered') and stores edges as EdgeSource::CoOccurrence, making them indistinguishable from co-occurrence edges.
Benchmark impact is minimal (<5% of LOCOMO queries are causal), so this is a production quality improvement, not a metrics-moving feature.
